This problem.

Among these five, one hundred applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.

For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for, default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic, problem of overfitting.

We know that the color of the shirts should have no, no inference with whether he's going to default or not.

aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

bullets.ai_comment: This section introduces the practical challenges encountered when training neural networks, motivated by a hands-on observation: a six-layer model may actually perform worse than a simpler two-layer model, which seems counterintuitive given that deeper networks are theoretically more powerful. The teacher uses this surprising result as the entry point to discuss real-world training pitfalls. Understanding these practical aspects is essential because theoretical depth alone does not guarantee better performance — proper training techniques are equally critical.

generated_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate.
This problem.
And you are given the files of one hundred applicants.
Among these five, one hundred applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a practical prediction problem: determining which loan applicants will repay and which will default. This is framed as a real-world supervised learning scenario where historical data is used to make future predictions. The problem is relevant to the banking and finance industry, where accurately identifying risky borrowers can save institutions significant losses.

bullets.ppt_text: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher sets up the scenario where a lender (e.g., a bank) enlists a data scientist to investigate repayment behavior. The word 'investigate' implies analyzing patterns in existing applicant data to build a predictive model. This mirrors how financial institutions actually deploy machine learning models to automate and improve credit risk assessment.

bullets.ppt_text: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher specifies that you are provided with complete records on 100 loan applicants, which serve as your training dataset. These 'files' likely contain features such as income, credit history, employment status, and loan amount. Having complete files is important because missing data can significantly affect model performance and reliability.

Out of the 100 applicants, only 5 defaulted within a 3-year period, meaning the dataset is heavily imbalanced — 95 repaid and only 5 did not. The teacher highlights this as a key characteristic of the dataset, which is a common challenge in real-world classification problems. Class imbalance like this can cause models to be biased toward predicting the majority class, making techniques like oversampling or adjusted decision thresholds especially important.

A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a scenario where a model is tasked with predicting loan defaults. The 'surprising finding' refers to the discovery that all five people who defaulted shared a seemingly irrelevant trait — wearing blue shirts during interviews. This sets up a critical lesson about the dangers of blindly trusting what a model identifies as a strong signal. The example is designed to immediately trigger the listener's intuition that something is wrong with this conclusion.

bullets.ppt_text: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes a dataset where photos were provided, and upon analysis, every single one of the five defaulters happened to be wearing a blue shirt at their interview. The key issue the teacher highlights is that this is a coincidence born from an extremely small sample — you cannot conclude that blue shirts predict default just because all defaulters wore them, especially if non-defaulters also wore blue shirts. This is a textbook example of a spurious correlation, where two variables appear related but have no causal or logical connection.

bullets.ppt_text: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model, lacking human common sense, will statistically identify blue shirts as a strong predictive feature and treat it as legitimate. This is a classic symptom of overfitting — the model has memorized a pattern specific to the tiny training set rather than learning a generalizable rule. The teacher emphasizes that human logic tells us shirt color has no reasonable inference on financial behavior, which is why domain knowledge and critical thinking must always accompany model outputs, not just raw statistics.

• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data

aligned_segments.text: And this introduce our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
Training error means, the error on the training data set and generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represents the performance on a new data set.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces one of the most foundational concepts in machine learning: the distinction between how well a model performs on data it has already seen versus data it has never seen. The teacher frames this as a 'classic discussion,' signaling its central importance in understanding model behavior. Understanding this distinction is essential for diagnosing whether a model is underfitting or overfitting.

bullets.ppt_text: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: Training error is the error a model makes on the exact data it was trained on. The teacher explains it as 'the error on the training data set,' meaning it measures how well the model has learned the patterns it was directly exposed to. A very low training error can be misleading, as it does not tell us anything about how the model will perform on unseen data.

bullets.ppt_text: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error measures how well the model performs on data it has never seen before, which is the true test of a model's usefulness in practice. The teacher explicitly states that 'generalization error refers to your error on new data,' emphasizing that this is the metric that actually matters in real-world applications. A large gap between training error and generalization error is a key indicator of overfitting.

bullets.ppt_text: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher uses a student exam analogy to make the abstract concept intuitive: practicing with past exams is like training a model on a training dataset, while the future exam represents unseen, real-world data. The teacher begins introducing this analogy around the 04:55 mark, saying 'this is very similar to we make practices for a [future exam].' This analogy is powerful because it connects the statistical concept to a universally relatable experience.

bullets.ppt_text: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: This is the core takeaway of the analogy: low training error does not imply low generalization error. The teacher reinforces this by stating 'the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set.' This is a critical warning against over-optimizing for training performance and highlights why a separate test set is always needed to evaluate a model honestly.

• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
